title.en: ELECTRA vs BERT: Hands-On Pretraining Objective Showdown

description.en: Run side-by-side experiments comparing ELECTRA's replaced-token-detection against BERT's masked language modeling — probe per-token confidence, measure gradient coverage, and discover why RTD produces stronger representations with less compute across PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX, and Rust.

story_variants.en:
## Pretraining Showdown: Detecting Fakes vs Guessing Blanks

Vaathiyaar here! Everyone talks about how ELECTRA beats BERT at a fraction of the compute — but *how does it actually feel different when you probe both models?* Today we stop reading papers and start running experiments. We will load `google/electra-base-discriminator` and `bert-base-uncased` side by side, feed them identical sentences, and watch how their pretraining objectives shaped entirely different internal behaviors.

### Experiment 1: Probing ELECTRA's Token-Level Confidence

ELECTRA's discriminator outputs a raw logit for each token — positive means "I think this was replaced", negative means "looks original". Let's feed it clean text and see how confident it is:

```python
from transformers import ElectraForPreTraining, ElectraTokenizerFast
import torch

tokenizer = ElectraTokenizerFast.from_pretrained(
    "google/electra-base-discriminator"
)
disc = ElectraForPreTraining.from_pretrained(
    "google/electra-base-discriminator"
)

sentence = "the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog"
inputs = tokenizer(sentence, return_tensors="pt")
with torch.no_grad():
    logits = disc(**inputs).logits[0]

for tok, score in zip(
    tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(inputs["input_ids"][0]),
    logits
):
    verdict = "REPLACED" if score > 0 else "original"
    print(f"{tok:>10}  {score:+.3f}  {verdict}")
```

On clean text, all logits should be strongly negative — the discriminator is confident every token is real.

### Experiment 2: BERT Sees Blanks, ELECTRA Sees Everything

The fundamental difference: BERT learns only from masked positions, while ELECTRA's discriminator classifies every token.

| What the model sees | BERT (MLM) | ELECTRA (RTD) |
|---|---|---|
| Input | `the [MASK] brown [MASK] jumps` | `the slow brown cat jumps` |
| Task at position 2 | Predict "quick" from vocab (30K softmax) | Binary: "slow" is replaced (sigmoid) |
| Task at position 4 | Predict "fox" from vocab (30K softmax) | Binary: "cat" is replaced (sigmoid) |
| Task at position 1 | No gradient — token is visible | Binary: "the" is original (sigmoid) |
| Task at position 5 | No gradient — token is visible | Binary: "jumps" is original (sigmoid) |
| Gradient positions | 2 out of 5 (40% with aggressive mask) | 5 out of 5 (100%) |

### Experiment 3: Representation Quality Comparison

Because ELECTRA trains on all tokens, its contextual embeddings capture richer token-level semantics:

```python
from transformers import ElectraModel, BertModel, AutoTokenizer
import torch
from torch.nn.functional import cosine_similarity

def get_cls_embedding(model_name, text):
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
    model = (ElectraModel if "electra" in model_name
             else BertModel).from_pretrained(model_name)
    inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.no_grad():
        out = model(**inputs)
    return out.last_hidden_state[:, 0, :]

text_a = "the bank approved the loan"
text_b = "the river bank was steep"

for name in ["google/electra-base-discriminator",
             "bert-base-uncased"]:
    emb_a = get_cls_embedding(name, text_a)
    emb_b = get_cls_embedding(name, text_b)
    sim = cosine_similarity(emb_a, emb_b).item()
    print(f"{name.split('/')[-1]:>30}: {sim:.4f}")
```

ELECTRA typically produces lower similarity for semantically different sentences — its per-token training makes it more sensitive to meaning shifts.

### Why This Matters for Fine-Tuning

BERT's `[MASK]` token never appears during fine-tuning — a distribution shift the model must overcome. ELECTRA never uses `[MASK]`, so its learned representations transfer cleanly:

```python
from transformers import ElectraForSequenceClassification

model = ElectraForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    "google/electra-base-discriminator", num_labels=3
)
# No [MASK] mismatch — discriminator representations
# transfer directly to downstream classification
print(f"Classifier params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters()):,}")
```

> **Key Insight:** The gap between ELECTRA and BERT is not just about efficiency numbers on a slide — it is observable in how the models *behave*. ELECTRA's discriminator produces sharper per-token logits, distinguishes semantic nuance better in embeddings, and transfers to downstream tasks without the [MASK]-token distribution shift. When you probe both models side by side, the 100%-vs-15% gradient coverage difference becomes viscerally real.

animation_sequence.content: BERT masks 15% of tokens and predicts them — but wastes 85% of each training example. ELECTRA replaces tokens with plausible fakes from a generator, then trains its discriminator to classify EVERY token as real or replaced. Today we run side-by-side experiments on both models to see how this pretraining difference manifests in actual model behavior.

animation_sequence.code:
from transformers import ElectraForPreTraining
from transformers import ElectraTokenizerFast
import torch

tokenizer = ElectraTokenizerFast.from_pretrained(
    "google/electra-base-discriminator"
)
disc = ElectraForPreTraining.from_pretrained(
    "google/electra-base-discriminator"
)

clean = "the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog"
inputs = tokenizer(clean, return_tensors="pt")

with torch.no_grad():
    clean_logits = disc(**inputs).logits[0]

fake = "the quick brown cat jumps over the lazy dog"
fake_inputs = tokenizer(fake, return_tensors="pt")
with torch.no_grad():
    fake_logits = disc(**fake_inputs).logits[0]

tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(
    fake_inputs["input_ids"][0]
)
for tok, c, f in zip(tokens, clean_logits, fake_logits):
    shift = f - c
    print(f"{tok:>8}  clean={c:+.2f}  fake={f:+.2f}  shift={shift:+.2f}")

steps.explanation: Import ElectraForPreTraining — this includes the RTD head that outputs per-token logits for 'original' vs 'replaced' classification. This is the model as it was pretrained, not stripped for fine-tuning.
The Fast tokenizer uses a Rust backend for ~5x faster encoding. ELECTRA shares BERT's 30,522-token WordPiece vocabulary.
Load the pretrained tokenizer. This handles [CLS]/[SEP] insertion, subword splitting, and attention mask generation identically to BERT.
Load the full discriminator with its RTD head — 110M transformer parameters plus a tiny 768-param linear layer that maps each token's hidden state to a single logit.
Start with a clean sentence — all tokens are original. We expect the discriminator to assign strongly negative logits (confident 'original') to every position.
Tokenize the clean sentence. The model receives input_ids, attention_mask, and token_type_ids — same tensor format as BERT.
Run inference with no gradients. The forward pass flows through 12 transformer layers, then the RTD head produces one logit per token.
Extract clean-sentence logits. Negative values = 'original' prediction. We save these as a baseline to compare against the corrupted version.
Create a corrupted version: 'fox' replaced with 'cat'. This simulates what the generator would produce during pretraining — a plausible but incorrect substitution.
Print each token with its clean score, fake score, and the shift. The replaced token ('cat' where 'fox' was) should show a large positive shift — the discriminator detected the fake.

animation_sequence.code:
def gradient_coverage_comparison(
    seq_len, mask_ratio, n_batches
):
    bert_signals_per_batch = round(seq_len * mask_ratio)
    electra_signals_per_batch = seq_len

    bert_total = bert_signals_per_batch * n_batches
    electra_total = electra_signals_per_batch * n_batches

    multiplier = electra_total / bert_total
    wasted_by_bert = electra_total - bert_total

    return {
        "bert_signals_per_example": bert_signals_per_batch,
        "electra_signals_per_example": electra_signals_per_batch,
        "bert_total": bert_total,
        "electra_total": electra_total,
        "signal_multiplier": round(multiplier, 2),
        "wasted_signals": wasted_by_bert,
    }

stats = gradient_coverage_comparison(
    seq_len=512, mask_ratio=0.15, n_batches=10000
)
for key, val in stats.items():
    print(f"{key}: {val}")

steps.explanation: Define a function that makes BERT's wasted training signal concrete — we calculate how many token-level learning signals each approach generates over a training run.
BERT's MLM only computes loss on masked tokens: round(512 * 0.15) = 77 signals per example. The other 435 tokens pass through without contributing to learning.
ELECTRA's RTD computes loss on every token: all 512 produce a gradient signal. This is the fundamental advantage — zero wasted tokens.
Over 10,000 batches: BERT generates 770,000 training signals, while ELECTRA generates 5,120,000 from the same data.
The multiplier: ELECTRA gets ~6.65x more training signals per token processed. This compounds over pretraining into a massive efficiency advantage.
Wasted signals: 4,350,000 token positions where BERT saw the correct token but learned nothing — ELECTRA would have extracted a binary classification signal from each one.
Run with realistic pretraining parameters and print the full comparison. These numbers explain why electra-base matches bert-large on GLUE benchmarks.

animation_sequence.message: You have run hands-on experiments comparing ELECTRA's replaced-token-detection against BERT's masked language modeling — and seen the 6.67x signal advantage, sharper per-token confidence, and cleaner fine-tuning transfer in action!

instruction.en: Build a `PretrainingProbe` class that simulates and compares both pretraining objectives on the same input. Implement: (1) `run_mlm(tokens, mask_ratio, seed)` — masks `round(len(tokens) * mask_ratio)` random positions (replacing with '[MASK]'), returns a dict with 'masked_input' (list of tokens with [MASK] inserted), 'loss_positions' (set of indices that contribute to loss), and 'gradient_coverage' (fraction of tokens with gradient signal = len(loss_positions)/len(tokens)). (2) `run_rtd(tokens, fake_vocab, mask_ratio, seed)` — replaces the same number of positions with random words from fake_vocab, returns a dict with 'corrupted_input', 'labels' (list of 'original'/'replaced' per position), 'loss_positions' (set of ALL indices — every token contributes), and 'gradient_coverage' (always 1.0). (3) `compare(tokens, fake_vocab, mask_ratio, seed)` — runs both and returns a dict with 'mlm_coverage', 'rtd_coverage', and 'signal_ratio' (rtd/mlm coverage). Track `self.experiments_run` across all calls to run_mlm, run_rtd, and compare (compare counts as 1 experiment, not 2).

practice_challenges.starter_code:
import random

class PretrainingProbe:
    def __init__(self):
        self.experiments_run = 0

    def run_mlm(self, tokens: list[str], mask_ratio: float,
                seed: int) -> dict:
        # Mask round(len(tokens)*mask_ratio) random positions
        # Replace selected positions with '[MASK]'
        # Only masked positions produce loss (gradient signal)
        # Increment experiments_run
        pass

    def run_rtd(self, tokens: list[str],
                fake_vocab: list[str],
                mask_ratio: float,
                seed: int) -> dict:
        # Replace round(len(tokens)*mask_ratio) random positions
        # with words from fake_vocab
        # ALL positions produce loss (gradient signal)
        # Increment experiments_run
        pass

    def compare(self, tokens: list[str],
                fake_vocab: list[str],
                mask_ratio: float,
                seed: int) -> dict:
        # Run both probes and return coverage comparison
        # This counts as 1 experiment, not 2
        # Increment experiments_run ONCE
        pass


# Test:
probe = PretrainingProbe()
tokens = ["the", "chef", "cooked", "a", "delicious", "meal"]

mlm = probe.run_mlm(tokens, 0.5, seed=42)
print(mlm['masked_input'].count('[MASK]'))
print(f"{mlm['gradient_coverage']:.2f}")

rtd = probe.run_rtd(tokens, ["cat", "dog", "fish"], 0.5, seed=42)
print(rtd['labels'].count('replaced'))
print(f"{rtd['gradient_coverage']:.2f}")

cmp = probe.compare(tokens, ["cat", "dog", "fish"], 0.15, seed=7)
print(f"{cmp['signal_ratio']:.2f}")
print(probe.experiments_run)

en: In run_mlm, use rng = random.Random(seed), select positions with rng.sample(), replace those with '[MASK]', and set loss_positions to only those masked indices. gradient_coverage = len(loss_positions) / len(tokens)
In run_rtd, use the same seed logic, but loss_positions = set(range(len(tokens))) because ELECTRA's discriminator classifies EVERY token. gradient_coverage is always 1.0

In compare, call run_mlm and run_rtd internally BUT decrement experiments_run by 1 so the net effect is +1 (since each sub-call increments). Or track separately — just ensure compare adds exactly 1 to experiments_run

practice_challenges.test_code:
p = PretrainingProbe()
t = ["a", "b", "c", "d", "e", "f", "g", "h", "i", "j"]
m = p.run_mlm(t, 0.3, seed=5)
assert m['masked_input'].count('[MASK]') == 3
assert len(m['loss_positions']) == 3
assert abs(m['gradient_coverage'] - 0.3) < 0.01
assert all(m['masked_input'][i] == '[MASK]' for i in m['loss_positions'])
assert all(m['masked_input'][i] == t[i] for i in range(10) if i not in m['loss_positions'])
r = p.run_rtd(t, ["x", "y"], 0.3, seed=5)
assert r['labels'].count('replaced') == 3
assert r['labels'].count('original') == 7
assert len(r['loss_positions']) == 10
assert abs(r['gradient_coverage'] - 1.0) < 0.01
assert all(r['corrupted_input'][i] in ['x','y'] for i in range(10) if r['labels'][i] == 'replaced')
c = p.compare(t, ["x", "y"], 0.2, seed=1)
assert 'mlm_coverage' in c and 'rtd_coverage' in c and 'signal_ratio' in c
assert abs(c['rtd_coverage'] - 1.0) < 0.01
assert abs(c['signal_ratio'] - c['rtd_coverage']/c['mlm_coverage']) < 0.01
assert p.experiments_run == 3

instruction.en: Build a `TokenDetectionBenchmark` class that evaluates a simulated ELECTRA discriminator's ability to spot replaced tokens across multiple sentences. The discriminator is an oracle — it compares each token against the original and flags mismatches. Implement: (1) `corrupt_sentence(tokens, fake_vocab, mask_ratio, seed)` returns (corrupted_tokens, ground_truth_labels) where labels are 'replaced'/'original'. (2) `oracle_discriminate(original, corrupted)` returns predictions: 'replaced' if tokens differ, else 'original'. (3) `evaluate(original, predictions, ground_truth)` returns a dict with 'accuracy', 'true_positives' (correctly identified replacements), 'false_positives' (original tokens wrongly flagged), and 'total_tokens'. (4) `run_benchmark(sentences, fake_vocab, mask_ratio, seed)` runs the full pipeline on multiple sentences (incrementing seed per sentence) and returns aggregate metrics. Track `self.total_sentences` and `self.total_tokens`.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
import random

class TokenDetectionBenchmark:
    def __init__(self):
        self.total_sentences = 0
        self.total_tokens = 0

    def corrupt_sentence(self, tokens: list[str],
                          fake_vocab: list[str],
                          mask_ratio: float,
                          seed: int
                          ) -> tuple[list[str], list[str]]:
        # Replace round(len(tokens)*mask_ratio) positions
        # with random fake_vocab words
        # Return (corrupted, ground_truth_labels)
        pass

    def oracle_discriminate(self, original: list[str],
                             corrupted: list[str]
                             ) -> list[str]:
        # Compare token by token: 'replaced' if different,
        # 'original' if same
        pass

    def evaluate(self, original: list[str],
                  predictions: list[str],
                  ground_truth: list[str]) -> dict:
        # accuracy: fraction of correct predictions
        # true_positives: count of correctly spotted replacements
        # false_positives: count of originals wrongly flagged
        # total_tokens: len(original)
        pass

    def run_benchmark(self, sentences: list[list[str]],
                       fake_vocab: list[str],
                       mask_ratio: float,
                       seed: int) -> dict:
        # Run corrupt -> discriminate -> evaluate per sentence
        # Increment seed by 1 for each sentence
        # Update self.total_sentences and self.total_tokens
        # Return aggregate: total_accuracy, total_tp, total_fp,
        #                    total_tokens
        pass


# Test:
bench = TokenDetectionBenchmark()

toks = ["the", "big", "red", "fox", "ran", "fast"]
corrupted, gt = bench.corrupt_sentence(
    toks, ["cat", "dog"], 0.5, seed=42
)
preds = bench.oracle_discriminate(toks, corrupted)
metrics = bench.evaluate(toks, preds, gt)

print(len(corrupted))
print(gt.count('replaced'))
print(f"{metrics['accuracy']:.2f}")
print(metrics['false_positives'])

sentences = [
    ["I", "like", "dogs"],
    ["the", "sun", "shines"]
]
result = bench.run_benchmark(
    sentences, ["x", "y", "z"], 0.33, seed=0
)
print(bench.total_sentences)
print(bench.total_tokens)

en: In corrupt_sentence, use rng = random.Random(seed), rng.sample for position selection, and rng.choice for replacements. Labels: 'replaced' if index was selected, else 'original'

The oracle discriminator compares token-by-token: if corrupted[i] != original[i] predict 'replaced'. This can be wrong when the generator randomly picks the original word — predicting 'original' for a corrupted position
In run_benchmark, loop over sentences with enumerate, use seed+i for each. Aggregate true_positives, false_positives, total_tokens across sentences, then compute total_accuracy from those

practice_challenges.test_code:
b = TokenDetectionBenchmark()
t = ["a", "b", "c", "d", "e"]
c, gt = b.corrupt_sentence(t, ["x", "y"], 0.4, seed=10)
assert len(c) == 5
assert gt.count('replaced') == 2
assert gt.count('original') == 3
assert all(c[i] in ['x','y'] for i in range(5) if gt[i] == 'replaced')
p = b.oracle_discriminate(t, c)
assert len(p) == 5
assert all(v in ['replaced','original'] for v in p)
ev = b.evaluate(t, p, gt)
assert 'accuracy' in ev and 'true_positives' in ev and 'false_positives' in ev
assert ev['false_positives'] == 0
assert 0.0 <= ev['accuracy'] <= 1.0
ss = [["m","n","o","p"], ["q","r","s","t","u"]]
res = b.run_benchmark(ss, ["z"], 0.5, seed=0)
assert b.total_sentences == 2
assert b.total_tokens == 9
assert 'total_accuracy' in res and 'total_tp' in res and 'total_fp' in res
